title: Rabbit nutrition and feeding
subtitle: What rabbits need to eat, in what proportions, and what to avoid.
excerpt: Hay is not a supplement for rabbits. It is the meal. Everything else is the supplement. Getting this ratio right prevents the digestive and dental problems that shorten most pet and smallholding rabbits' lives.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council, nutrition guidelines. RSPCA, rabbit welfare and feeding guidance. RWAF (Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund), dietary recommendations.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soft, grape-like pellets produced in the rabbit's caecum and eaten directly from the anus, usually at night or in early morning. They are rich in protein and B vitamins and are a normal, essential part of the rabbit's diet. A rabbit that is too fat to reach its hindquarters, or whose diet is already too rich, may leave caecotrophs uneaten — this is a sign that the diet needs correcting.

A condition in which the rabbit's digestive system slows or stops. Can develop rapidly and become life-threatening within 24 hours. Causes include insufficient fibre, stress, pain, or dehydration. Signs include a hunched posture, no droppings, a hard or bloated abdomen, and refusal to eat.

content.text: A rabbit's digestive system is designed to process large amounts of fibrous material continuously. The fibre keeps gut motility going and wears the teeth, which grow throughout life. A diet too low in fibre and too high in starch leads to
and dental overgrowth, the two most common preventable causes of rabbit death.

Good-quality hay or dried grass should make up approximately 80 to 90 per cent of a rabbit's daily diet by volume, and should be available at all times. Timothy hay is appropriate for most adult rabbits; meadow hay is also fine. Alfalfa hay is too high in calcium for most adults and is better suited to young growing rabbits. The hay should smell fresh and not dusty. A rabbit eating less hay than usual is a sign something is wrong.

A daily serving of fresh leafy greens provides moisture and variety. Suitable options include dark leafy greens (kale, spring greens, bok choy), fresh herbs (parsley, basil, coriander), and dandelion leaves. Introduce new greens gradually to avoid loose droppings. Iceberg lettuce has minimal nutritional value and can cause loose stools; avoid it. Avoid feeding large quantities of spinach or brassicas daily as these are high in oxalates or goitrogens.

Pellets, if used at all, should be a small measured daily portion, not a free-choice supply. A small adult rabbit needs a tablespoon or two per day at most. Muesli-style mixed feeds allow selective feeding (the sweet bits get eaten first, the fibrous bits left) and should be avoided. Plain timothy-based pellets are the best choice if you feed any concentrate at all.
Rabbits produce two types of dropping: hard, round faecal pellets and soft, clustered
. If you find clusters of soft droppings left in the hutch uneaten, the diet is probably too rich in concentrate and not high enough in hay. Reduce pellets and increase hay.

Do not feed: bread, pasta, crackers, or any starchy human food; potatoes; rhubarb; onion family plants; avocado; chocolate; seeds or nuts. Fresh fruit can be offered as an occasional treat in very small quantities but is high in sugar and should not be a daily item.
